revision.text:
{{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=1.15|lon_deg=140|lon_min=34|lon_sec=32.82|label=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|position=bottom|width=220|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|caption={{Center|Location of Kinouchi Temple Ruins}}}} '''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an [[Ancient]] [[temple]] site located in {{ill|Katori, Chiba|ja|香取市}}, [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.
== Overview == [[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]] The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the {{ill|Tone River|ja|利根川}} (formerly {{ill|Kinu River|ja|鬼怒川}}), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.
The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[7th century]] during the [[Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[9th century]] in the [[Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
== Site History == * [[1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. * [[1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction. * [[1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.
== Notes == {{Notes help}} {{reflist}}
== References == {{small|(Sources used for article composition)}} * Site information board * {{Cite book|lang=ja|year=1996|chapter=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|title={{ill|Japan Historical Place Name Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 12: Chiba Prefecture Place Names|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490085|ref={{Harvid|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Heibonsha)|1996}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|year=1998|chapter=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|title=Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3 (Prefectural History Series 11)|publisher=Chiba Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}}}
== Related Literature == {{small|(Literature not used in article composition)}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor=Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team|year=1981|title=Preliminary Report on the Excavation of the Kinouchi Temple Ruins - Katori District, Chiba Prefecture|publisher=Chiba Prefecture Board of Education}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} * {{ill|Shimokaijo Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|下海上国造}} * {{ill|Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)}} * {{ill|Kinouchi Daijin (Katori)|ja|木内大神 (香取市)}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] - Katori City website

revision.text:
{{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=1.15|lon_deg=140|lon_min=34|lon_sec=32.82|label=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|position=bottom|width=220|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|caption={{Center|Location of Kinouchi Temple Ruins}}}} '''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an [[Ancient]] [[temple]] site located in {{ill|Katori, Chiba|ja|香取市}}, [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.
== Overview == [[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]] The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the {{ill|Tone River|ja|利根川}} (formerly {{ill|Kinu River|ja|鬼怒川}}), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.
The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[7th century]] during the [[Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[9th century]] in the [[Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
== Site History == * [[1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. * [[1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction. * [[1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.
== Notes == {{Notes help}} {{reflist}}
== References == {{small|(Sources used for article composition)}} * Site information board * {{Cite book|lang=ja|year=1996|chapter=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|title={{ill|Japan Historical Place Name Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 12: Chiba Prefecture Place Names|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490085|ref={{Harvid|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Heibonsha)|1996}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|year=1998|chapter=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|title=Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3 (Prefectural History Series 11)|publisher=Chiba Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}}}
== Related Literature == {{small|(Literature not used in article composition)}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor=Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team|year=1981|title=Preliminary Report on the Excavation of the Kinouchi Temple Ruins - Katori District, Chiba Prefecture|publisher=Chiba Prefecture Board of Education}}== See Also == * {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} * {{ill|Shimokaijo Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|下海上国造}} * {{ill|Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)}} * {{ill|Kinouchi Daijin (Katori)|ja|木内大神 (香取市)}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] - Katori City website

It is not designated as a historic site.

Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.
The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 6]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=1.15|lon_deg=140|lon_min=34|lon_sec=32.82|label=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|position=bottom|width=220|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|caption={{Center|Location of Kinouchi Temple Ruins}}}} '''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an [[Ancient]] [[temple]] site located in {{ill|Katori, Chiba|ja|香取市}}, [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.
== Overview == [[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]] The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the {{ill|Tone River|ja|利根川}} (formerly {{ill|Kinu River|ja|鬼怒川}}), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.
The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[7th century]] during the [[Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[9th century]] in the [[Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
== Site History == * [[1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. * [[1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction. * [[1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.
== Notes == {{Notes help}} {{reflist}}
== References == {{small|(Sources used for article composition)}} * Site information board * {{Cite book|lang=ja|year=1996|chapter=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|title={{ill|Japan Historical Place Name Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 12: Chiba Prefecture Place Names|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490085|ref={{Harvid|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Heibonsha)|1996}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|year=1998|chapter=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|title=Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3 (Prefectural History Series 11)|publisher=Chiba Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}}}
== Related Literature == {{small|(Literature not used in article composition)}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor=Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team|year=1981|title=Preliminary Report on the Excavation of the Kinouchi Temple Ruins - Katori District, Chiba Prefecture|publisher=Chiba Prefecture Board of Education}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} * {{ill|Shimokaijo Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|下海上国造}} * {{ill|Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)}} * {{ill|Kinouchi Daijin (Katori)|ja|木内大神 (香取市)}}

{{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=1.15|lon_deg=140|lon_min=34|lon_sec=32.82|label=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|position=bottom|width=220|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|caption={{Center|Location of Kinouchi Temple Ruins}}}} '''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an [[Ancient]] [[temple]] site located in {{ill|Katori, Chiba|ja|香取市|12=draft}}, [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.
== Overview == [[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]] The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the {{ill|Tone River|ja|利根川|12=draft}} (formerly {{ill|Kinu River|ja|鬼怒川|12=draft}}), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.
The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[7th century]] during the [[Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[9th century]] in the [[Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=draft}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡|12=draft}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
== Site History == * [[1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. * [[1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction. * [[1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=draft}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.
== Notes == {{Notes help}} {{reflist}}
== References == {{small|(Sources used for article composition)}} * Site information board * {{Cite book|lang=ja|year=1996|chapter=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|title={{ill|Japan Historical Place Name Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=draft}} Vol. 12: Chiba Prefecture Place Names|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}|isbn=4582490085|ref={{Harvid|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Heibonsha)|1996}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|year=1998|chapter=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|title=Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3 (Prefectural History Series 11)|publisher=Chiba Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}}}

{{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=1.15|lon_deg=140|lon_min=34|lon_sec=32.82|label=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|position=bottom|width=220|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|caption={{Center|Location of Kinouchi Temple Ruins}}}}
'''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] site located in [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori, Chiba]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.

== Overview ==
[[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]]
The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] (formerly [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinu River]]), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[:en:1981|1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.

The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[:en:7th century|7th century]] during the [[:en:Hakuhō period|Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[:en:9th century|9th century]] in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijo District]] during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* [[:en:1956|1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* [[:en:1980|1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* [[:en:1981|1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[:en:7th century|7th century]] during the [[:en:Hakuhō period|Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[:en:9th century|9th century]] in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijo District]] during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* [[:en:1956|1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* [[:en:1980|1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* [[:en:1981|1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

'''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] site located in [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori, Chiba]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.

== Overview ==
[[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]]
The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] (formerly [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinu River]]), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[:en:1981|1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.

The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[:en:7th century|7th century]] during the [[:en:Hakuhō period|Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[:en:9th century|9th century]] in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijo District]] during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* [[:en:1956|1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* [[:en:1980|1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* [[:en:1981|1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

{{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=1.15|lon_deg=140|lon_min=34|lon_sec=32.82|label=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|position=bottom|width=220|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|caption={{Center|Location of Kinouchi Temple Ruins}}}}
'''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] site located in [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori, Chiba]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.

== Overview ==
[[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]]
The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] (formerly [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinu River]]), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[:en:1981|1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.

The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[:en:7th century|7th century]] during the [[:en:Hakuhō period|Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[:en:9th century|9th century]] in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijo District]] during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* [[:en:1956|1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* [[:en:1980|1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* [[:en:1981|1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

{{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=1.15|lon_deg=140|lon_min=34|lon_sec=32.82|label=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|position=bottom|width=220|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|caption={{Center|Location of Kinouchi Temple Ruins}}}}
'''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] site located in [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori, Chiba]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.

== Overview ==
[[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]]
The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] (formerly [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinu River]]), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[:en:1981|1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.

The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[:en:7th century|7th century]] during the [[:en:Hakuhō period|Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[:en:9th century|9th century]] in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijo District]] during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* [[:en:1956|1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* [[:en:1980|1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* [[:en:1981|1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

{{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=1.15|lon_deg=140|lon_min=34|lon_sec=32.82|label=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|position=bottom|width=220|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|caption={{Center|Location of Kinouchi Temple Ruins}}}}
'''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] site located in [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori, Chiba]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.

== Overview ==
[[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]]
The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] (formerly [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinu River]]), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[:en:1981|1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.

The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[:en:7th century|7th century]] during the [[:en:Hakuhō period|Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[:en:9th century|9th century]] in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijo District]] during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* [[:en:1956|1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* [[:en:1980|1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* [[:en:1981|1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

== Notes ==

* {{Cite book|lang=ja|year=1998|chapter=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|title=Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3 (Prefectural History Series 11)|publisher=Chiba Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}}}

'''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] site located in [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori, Chiba]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.

== Overview ==
[[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]]
The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] (formerly [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinu River]]), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[:en:1981|1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.

The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[:en:7th century|7th century]] during the [[:en:Hakuhō period|Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[:en:9th century|9th century]] in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijo District]] during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* [[:en:1956|1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* [[:en:1980|1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* [[:en:1981|1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[:en:7th century|7th century]] during the [[:en:Hakuhō period|Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[:en:9th century|9th century]] in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijo District]] during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* [[:en:1956|1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* [[:en:1980|1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* [[:en:1981|1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

'''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] site located in [[:en:Katori, Chiba|Katori, Chiba]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.

== Overview ==
[[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]]
The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] (formerly [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinu River]]), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in [[:en:1981|1981]] after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.

The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late [[:en:7th century|7th century]] during the [[:en:Hakuhō period|Hakuhō period]] and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-[[:en:9th century|9th century]] in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijo District]] during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* [[:en:1956|1956]]: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* [[:en:1980|1980]]: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* [[:en:1981|1981]]: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in {{ill|1981|en|1981|WD=Q2437|lt=1981}} after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.

The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}} during the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period}} and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-{{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijō District, Chiba|en|Kaijō District, Chiba|WD=Q6347746|lt=Kaijo District}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* {{ill|1956|en|1956|WD=Q5221|lt=1956}}: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* {{ill|1980|en|1980|WD=Q2439|lt=1980}}: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* {{ill|1981|en|1981|WD=Q2437|lt=1981}}: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}} during the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period}} and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-{{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijō District, Chiba|en|Kaijō District, Chiba|WD=Q6347746|lt=Kaijo District}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* {{ill|1956|en|1956|WD=Q5221|lt=1956}}: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* {{ill|1980|en|1980|WD=Q2439|lt=1980}}: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* {{ill|1981|en|1981|WD=Q2437|lt=1981}}: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

{{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=1.15|lon_deg=140|lon_min=34|lon_sec=32.82|label=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|position=bottom|width=220|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|caption={{Center|Location of Kinouchi Temple Ruins}}}}
'''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=Ancient history}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple}} site located in {{ill|Katori, Chiba|en|Katori, Chiba|WD=Q858357|lt=Katori, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}}, Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.

== Overview ==
[[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]]
The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the {{ill|Tone River|en|Tone River|WD=Q177452|lt=Tone River}} (formerly {{ill|Kinugawa River|en|Kinugawa River|WD=Q1042040|lt=Kinu River}}), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in {{ill|1981|en|1981|WD=Q2437|lt=1981}} after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.

The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}} during the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period}} and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-{{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijō District, Chiba|en|Kaijō District, Chiba|WD=Q6347746|lt=Kaijo District}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* {{ill|1956|en|1956|WD=Q5221|lt=1956}}: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* {{ill|1980|en|1980|WD=Q2439|lt=1980}}: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* {{ill|1981|en|1981|WD=Q2437|lt=1981}}: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}} during the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period|1=Hakuhō period}} and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-{{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century|1=9th century}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijō District, Chiba|en|Kaijō District, Chiba|WD=Q6347746|lt=Kaijo District|1=Kaijō District, Chiba}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* {{ill|1956|en|1956|WD=Q5221|lt=1956|1=1956}}: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* {{ill|1980|en|1980|WD=Q2439|lt=1980|1=1980}}: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* {{ill|1981|en|1981|WD=Q2437|lt=1981|1=1981}}: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

{{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=1.15|lon_deg=140|lon_min=34|lon_sec=32.82|label=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|position=bottom|width=220|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|caption={{Center|Location of Kinouchi Temple Ruins}}}}
'''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=Ancient history|1=Ancient history}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple|1=Temple}} site located in {{ill|Katori, Chiba|en|Katori, Chiba|WD=Q858357|lt=Katori, Chiba|1=Katori, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.

== Overview ==
[[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]]
The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the {{ill|Tone River|en|Tone River|WD=Q177452|lt=Tone River|1=Tone River}} (formerly {{ill|Kinugawa River|en|Kinugawa River|WD=Q1042040|lt=Kinu River|1=Kinugawa River}}), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in {{ill|1981|en|1981|WD=Q2437|lt=1981|1=1981}} after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.

The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}} during the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period|1=Hakuhō period}} and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-{{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century|1=9th century}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijō District, Chiba|en|Kaijō District, Chiba|WD=Q6347746|lt=Kaijo District|1=Kaijō District, Chiba}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* {{ill|1956|en|1956|WD=Q5221|lt=1956|1=1956}}: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* {{ill|1980|en|1980|WD=Q2439|lt=1980|1=1980}}: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* {{ill|1981|en|1981|WD=Q2437|lt=1981|1=1981}}: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}} during the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period|1=Hakuhō period}} and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-{{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century|1=9th century}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijō District, Chiba|en|Kaijō District, Chiba|WD=Q6347746|lt=Kaijo District|1=Kaijō District, Chiba}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* {{ill|1956|en|1956|WD=Q5221|lt=1956|1=1956}}: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* {{ill|1980|en|1980|WD=Q2439|lt=1980|1=1980}}: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* {{ill|1981|en|1981|WD=Q2437|lt=1981|1=1981}}: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.
