{{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=1.15|lon_deg=140|lon_min=34|lon_sec=32.82|label=Kinouchi Temple Ruins|position=bottom|width=220|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|caption={{Center|Location of Kinouchi Temple Ruins}}}}
'''Kinouchi Temple Ruins''' (木内廃寺跡) is an {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=Ancient history|1=Ancient history}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple|1=Temple}} site located in {{ill|Katori, Chiba|en|Katori, Chiba|WD=Q858357|lt=Katori, Chiba|1=Katori, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, Japan. It is not designated as a historic site.

== Overview ==
[[File:木内廃寺跡 出土品.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Excavated Artifacts}}{{small|Displayed at Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum.}}]]
The site is located in the northeastern part of Chiba Prefecture, on a plateau on the west bank of the Kurobe River, south of the lower reaches of the {{ill|Tone River|en|Tone River|WD=Q177452|lt=Tone River|1=Tone River}} (formerly {{ill|Kinugawa River|en|Kinugawa River|WD=Q1042040|lt=Kinu River|1=Kinugawa River}}), at an elevation of 42 meters. Most of the site has been leveled, and an excavation survey was conducted in {{ill|1981|en|1981|WD=Q2437|lt=1981|1=1981}} after the foundation platform was exposed due to sand extraction.

The temple layout is not clearly understood, but a foundation platform, pit dwellings, and ditches have been identified. The foundation platform, except for the northwest corner, has been lost. It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}} during the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|WD=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period|1=Hakuhō period}} and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-{{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century|1=9th century}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijō District, Chiba|en|Kaijō District, Chiba|WD=Q6347746|lt=Kaijo District|1=Kaijō District, Chiba}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* {{ill|1956|en|1956|WD=Q5221|lt=1956|1=1956}}: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* {{ill|1980|en|1980|WD=Q2439|lt=1980|1=1980}}: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* {{ill|1981|en|1981|WD=Q2437|lt=1981|1=1981}}: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.

It was constructed using rammed earth with a rainwater drainage ditch, but decorative features and cornerstone stones were not detected{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Additionally, many roof tiles were excavated during road expansion work on the southern side, suggesting the presence of another foundation structure{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Ink-inscribed pottery was also found in two of the pit dwellings{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. Numerous roof tiles have been unearthed from the temple precincts, believed to have been produced at the nearby Shimizu-iri Tile Kiln Site<ref name="Katori City">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/culture_sport/bunkazai/isan/isan_vol051-060.html#cmsVol056 Katori Heritage Archive Vol.056 Kinouchi Temple Ruins] (Katori City website).</ref>. The round eaves tiles exhibit three styles with five types of designs: three types of Rokkakuji-style lotus petal patterns, a plain-edged six-petal lotus pattern, and a plain-edged single sixteen-petal lotus pattern. The flat eaves tiles display three patterns: triple arc design, symmetrical arabesque, and arabesque{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

The temple is estimated to have been founded around the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|qid=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}} during the {{ill|Hakuhō period|en|Hakuhō period|qid=Q373860|lt=Hakuhō period|1=Hakuhō period}} and is thought to have remained in existence until the mid-{{ill|9th century|en|9th century|qid=Q8083|lt=9th century|1=9th century}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}. It is considered the oldest temple in the area. Taking into account the nearby {{ill|Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Jōyama Kofun Group|lt=Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|ja|城山古墳群 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama Kofun Cluster (Katori)|qq=}}, it is presumed that the temple's establishment had close ties with the ruling clans of {{ill|Kaijō District, Chiba|en|Kaijō District, Chiba|qid=Q6347746|lt=Kaijo District|1=Kaijō District, Chiba}} during that era{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.

== Site History ==
* {{ill|1956|en|1956|qid=Q5221|lt=1956|1=1956}}: Introduced as "Kinomiya Temple Ruins" by Gen Saburo Hirano and Hiroshi Takiguchi{{Sfn|Kinouchi Temple Ruins (Chiba Prefecture History, Archaeological Volume 3)|1998}}.
* {{ill|1980|en|1980|qid=Q2439|lt=1980|1=1980}}: Foundation platform exposed during sand extraction.
* {{ill|1981|en|1981|qid=Q2437|lt=1981|1=1981}}: Excavation survey conducted by the Kinouchi Temple Ruins Excavation Team, with a preliminary report published the same year.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja|香取市文化財保存館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori City Cultural Property Preservation Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (located inside Omigawa Civic Center Ibuki-kan, Katori City) - Houses and displays artifacts excavated from the Kinouchi Temple Ruins.
